title: End-of-Billing-Cycle Dispute After Maximum Service Extraction Pattern
narrative: Cardholder maintains active subscription service (streaming, SaaS, meal kit) for 28-30 days of full billing cycle, frequently logging in and consuming content or receiving deliveries throughout period. Dispute filed 1-3 days before next renewal charge, claiming unauthorized recurring transaction despite 40+ platform logins or 8+ delivery confirmations during disputed month. Pattern shows card1_txn_count_30day dropping from 15-25 to zero immediately after dispute filing. Temporal signature reveals disputes consistently filed on days 28-30 of 30-day cycles across multiple merchants, with ProductCD='S' (subscription). Email domains remain consistent (P_emaildomain = R_emaildomain) indicating legitimate account access. Chargeback reason code 4853/13.1 (services not provided) despite activity logs contradicting claim. Average disputed amount $29-$79 per subscription, targeting retention-focused merchants reluctant to contest small chargebacks.
indicators: Dispute filed 28-30 days post-transaction, 1-3 days before next scheduled recurring charge
card1_txn_count_30day shows 15-25 logins/activities during disputed period, drops to zero post-dispute
Multiple disputes across 3+ subscription merchants (ProductCD='S') within 90-day window
Consistent dispute timing pattern: 85%+ filed on day 28-30 of billing cycles
TransactionAmt range $29-$79, targeting amounts below merchant dispute cost threshold ($100)
P_emaildomain = R_emaildomain with verified delivery confirmations or login timestamps during disputed service period
Chargeback reason codes 4853 or 13.1 despite merchant-provided usage logs contradicting 'service not received' claim
summary: Customer actively uses a subscription service for nearly the entire billing cycle (28-30 days with 15-25 logins or deliveries), then disputes the charge 1-3 days before the next renewal hits. This pattern repeats across multiple subscription merchants within 90 days, with disputes always timed at cycle end and amounts typically $29-$79.
reasoning: The fraudster maximizes value extraction by using the service fully, then falsely claims the charge was unauthorized or service not received to get a refund. Timing disputes just before renewal and keeping amounts low exploits merchants' cost-benefit calculations, as contesting these chargebacks often costs more than the transaction itself.
indicator_explanations: Dispute filed right before next billing hits, after full month of service
Customer actively used the service throughout disputed period then vanished
Same dispute playbook repeated across multiple subscription services recently
Timing shows deliberate pattern of disputing just before renewal charges
Amounts carefully selected to stay under merchant's fight-back threshold
Email matches billing address with proof customer accessed service throughout period
Claiming non-delivery despite merchant logs showing active usage entire month
